key_insight: Raising argon flow rate from 1600 to 1800 L/h during air plasma spraying of NiCrFeBSiC coatings produced thicker, denser coatings with better wear behavior.
engineering_relevance: Tuning spray-gas flow is a practical process lever for improving thermal-sprayed self-fluxing coatings without changing the base alloy chemistry.
method: Air plasma sprayed NiCrFeBSiC self-fluxing metal-ceramic coatings
Compared argon flow rates of 1600 and 1800 L/h
Evaluated microstructure, microhardness, adhesion, and ball-on-disk tribology
evidence_signal: At 1800 L/h, coating thickness roughly doubled, porosity roughly halved, and the friction coefficient dropped to about 0.107; microhardness was 1015.8 ± 81.5 HV and elastic modulus 203.9 ± 5.4 GPa.
watch_item: Results are based on only two argon flow settings, so broader process optimization is needed before generalizing to other spray conditions or geometries.
paper_title: EFFECT OF ARGON FLOW RATE DURING AIR PLASMA SPRAYING ON THE STRUCTURE AND PROPERTIES OF NICRFEBSIC COATINGS
